prompt: What does Microsoft Defender for Cloud's Secure Score represent?
choices: A percentage summarizing adherence to security recommendations
The count of active security incidents
The monthly cost of enabled Defender plans
correctAnswers: A percentage summarizing adherence to security recommendations
explanation: Secure Score is a single metric of how well the environment follows Defender for Cloud recommendations.

prompt: What does the Defender for Cloud asset Inventory show?
correctAnswers: All connected/monitored resources across environments
explanation: Inventory is a comprehensive, queryable view of monitored resources across Azure, AWS, and GCP.

prompt: Which standard does the free CSPM foundation evaluate configuration against by default?
correctAnswers: The Microsoft Cloud Security Benchmark (MCSB)
explanation: MCSB is applied by default; other standards (PCI-DSS, ISO 27001, etc.) are added on top of it.

prompt: Where do you enable additional regulatory standards for a subscription in Defender for Cloud?
correctAnswers: Regulatory compliance ▸ Manage compliance policies
explanation: Standards like ISO 27001 or PCI-DSS are toggled per scope under Regulatory compliance ▸ Manage compliance policies.

prompt: When would you add a custom standard in Defender for Cloud?
choices: When no built-in standard matches your organization's internal baseline
When you want to disable the Microsoft Cloud Security Benchmark
When enabling a Defender workload plan
correctAnswers: When no built-in standard matches your organization's internal baseline
explanation: Custom standards let you assess against your own requirements beyond the built-in options.

prompt: Which two non-Azure clouds can be onboarded to Defender for Cloud as connectors?
choices: Amazon Web Services (AWS) and Google Cloud Platform (GCP)
correctAnswers: Amazon Web Services (AWS) and Google Cloud Platform (GCP)
explanation: Defender for Cloud natively supports AWS and GCP multicloud connectors.

prompt: What does Microsoft Defender External Attack Surface Management (EASM) do?
choices: Continuously discovers and maps internet-facing assets from the outside in
Scans internal VMs via an installed agent
Encrypts data in transit between VNets
correctAnswers: Continuously discovers and maps internet-facing assets from the outside in
explanation: EASM provides outside-in discovery of internet-facing assets, surfacing unknown/forgotten exposure.

prompt: How does EASM's visibility direction differ from the Defender for Cloud Inventory?
choices: EASM looks from outside inward (attacker view); Inventory looks from inside (authenticated admin view)
Inventory only covers AWS and GCP
EASM requires an agent on every asset
correctAnswers: EASM looks from outside inward (attacker view); Inventory looks from inside (authenticated admin view)
explanation: EASM is outside-in; Inventory/Secure Score are inside-out. Complementary, opposite directions.

prompt: What is the key distinction between CSPM and CWP in Defender for Cloud?
choices: CSPM is the free posture/config foundation; CWP is paid threat-detection plans
CSPM is paid; CWP is free
correctAnswers: CSPM is the free posture/config foundation; CWP is paid threat-detection plans
explanation: CSPM answers 'are we configured securely?'; CWP answers 'is something attacking us right now?'.

prompt: What is the relationship between MCSB and additional regulatory standards like ISO 27001?
choices: MCSB is the default baseline; additional standards layer on top, not replace it
Only one can be active at a time
correctAnswers: MCSB is the default baseline; additional standards layer on top, not replace it
explanation: MCSB is always applied by default; regulatory standards are additive.

prompt: Which Defender for Cloud view shows pass/fail status per individual regulatory control?
correctAnswers: The Regulatory compliance dashboard
explanation: The regulatory compliance dashboard maps enabled standards to controls and their pass/fail status.

prompt: Why onboard an AWS account into Defender for Cloud?
choices: To extend the same Secure Score and compliance visibility to AWS alongside Azure
To migrate AWS workloads into Azure automatically
To replace AWS IAM with Azure RBAC
Because it is required before enabling any Azure Defender plan
correctAnswers: To extend the same Secure Score and compliance visibility to AWS alongside Azure
explanation: Multicloud connectors bring AWS/GCP under one posture-management view.

prompt: During an acquisition, why would a security team run Defender EASM against the target company?
choices: To surface unknown, forgotten, or shadow-IT internet-facing assets before attackers find them
To automatically patch all discovered vulnerabilities
To enable TDE on the target's databases
To assign Azure RBAC roles to the target's staff
correctAnswers: To surface unknown, forgotten, or shadow-IT internet-facing assets before attackers find them
explanation: EASM reveals external exposure that internal inventories miss — valuable during M&A due diligence.

prompt: Secure Score is dropping. Which action would most directly raise it?
choices: Remediating the security recommendations that carry the most weight
correctAnswers: Remediating the security recommendations that carry the most weight
explanation: Secure Score reflects adherence to recommendations; remediating high-impact recommendations raises it. The other actions add coverage/standards but don't directly raise the score by themselves.

prompt: Which capability is part of the FREE CSPM foundation rather than a paid plan?
correctAnswers: Secure Score and security recommendations
explanation: Secure Score, recommendations, inventory, and MCSB assessment are free CSPM; the others are paid CWP capabilities.

prompt: A team assumes enabling PCI-DSS in Defender for Cloud will replace the default benchmark. What is correct?
choices: MCSB remains applied; PCI-DSS is added alongside it
Only PCI-DSS controls will be evaluated afterward
MCSB is only for AWS, so PCI-DSS replaces it for Azure
correctAnswers: MCSB remains applied; PCI-DSS is added alongside it
explanation: Standards are additive; MCSB continues to apply by default alongside any regulatory standard you enable.

prompt: Which tool answers the question 'What internet-facing assets do we have that we might not know about?'
correctAnswers: Microsoft Defender EASM
explanation: EASM discovers external, internet-facing assets from an attacker's perspective, including unknown ones.

prompt: Which best describes the role of the asset Inventory in security operations?
choices: A queryable catalog of resources and their security posture across clouds
A list of only the alerts raised in the last 24 hours
The billing breakdown of Defender plans
A record of every user sign-in
correctAnswers: A queryable catalog of resources and their security posture across clouds
explanation: Inventory catalogs monitored resources (via Azure Resource Graph) with posture context, across Azure/AWS/GCP.

prompt: A CISO wants one compliance view spanning Azure, AWS, and GCP. What must be in place?
choices: Multicloud connectors for AWS and GCP in Defender for Cloud
A separate Sentinel workspace per cloud
An EASM scan of each cloud
A resource lock on each subscription
correctAnswers: Multicloud connectors for AWS and GCP in Defender for Cloud
explanation: Onboarding AWS/GCP connectors unifies Secure Score and compliance across all three clouds.

prompt: Which statement about CSPM vs CWP is FALSE?
choices: CWP is the free foundation, while CSPM is the set of paid plans
CWP focuses on active threat detection
correctAnswers: CWP is the free foundation, while CSPM is the set of paid plans
explanation: It's the reverse: CSPM is the free foundation; CWP (Defender plans) is paid.

prompt: Which activity is squarely CSPM (posture), not CWP (threat protection)?
choices: Reviewing and remediating a recommendation to enable encryption on a storage account
Responding to an alert about anomalous storage access
Investigating an EDR detection on a VM
correctAnswers: Reviewing and remediating a recommendation to enable encryption on a storage account
explanation: Acting on configuration recommendations is CSPM; the alert-driven activities are CWP.

prompt: What does 'assess compliance against a security framework' mean in Defender for Cloud?
choices: Mapping your environment's configuration to a standard's controls and reporting pass/fail
Blocking deployments that violate the standard
Encrypting data to meet the standard
correctAnswers: Mapping your environment's configuration to a standard's controls and reporting pass/fail
explanation: Compliance assessment maps configuration to control requirements and reports status; enforcement/remediation are separate concerns.

prompt: Which is NOT something the free CSPM foundation provides?
choices: EDR (endpoint detection and response) on servers
correctAnswers: EDR (endpoint detection and response) on servers
explanation: EDR comes with the paid Defender for Servers plan (CWP). Secure Score, recommendations, and inventory are free CSPM.

prompt: A resource keeps failing a control in the regulatory compliance dashboard. What is the intended remediation path?
choices: Follow the linked assessment/recommendation to fix the underlying configuration
Remove the standard so the control disappears
Ignore it, as compliance is informational only
correctAnswers: Follow the linked assessment/recommendation to fix the underlying configuration
explanation: Each control maps to assessments/recommendations you remediate to raise compliance — not by hiding the standard.

prompt: Which two are part of the free CSPM foundation? (Choose two.)
correctAnswers: Secure Score
Asset inventory
explanation: Secure Score and inventory are free CSPM; malware detection and agentless scanning are paid CWP capabilities.

prompt: Which two statements are correct? (Choose two.)
choices: The Microsoft Cloud Security Benchmark is applied by default
Custom standards can be added when built-in ones don't fit
Only one standard can be active on a subscription at a time
correctAnswers: The Microsoft Cloud Security Benchmark is applied by default
Custom standards can be added when built-in ones don't fit
explanation: MCSB stays active alongside additional standards, and multiple standards can be active at once.

prompt: Which two directions of visibility do Inventory and EASM respectively represent? (Choose two.)
choices: EASM = outside-in (attacker view of internet-facing assets)
EASM = requires an agent on each internal VM
correctAnswers: Inventory = inside-out (authenticated admin view)
EASM = outside-in (attacker view of internet-facing assets)
explanation: Inventory is the internal catalog; EASM is the external, agentless attacker-perspective discovery.

prompt: Referring to requirement 3 (one score summarizing configuration compliance across Azure, an AWS account, and a GCP project), what should Adatum configure?
choices: Onboard AWS and GCP as multicloud connectors in Defender for Cloud
Compile separate reports from each cloud's native tools
Migrate AWS/GCP workloads into Azure first
correctAnswers: Onboard AWS and GCP as multicloud connectors in Defender for Cloud
explanation: Multicloud connectors unify Secure Score/compliance across Azure, AWS, and GCP in one view.

prompt: Referring to requirement 4 (discover a subsidiary's internet-facing web servers the way an attacker would), which tool should Adatum use?
choices: Microsoft Defender External Attack Surface Management (EASM)
Defender for Cloud asset inventory alone
correctAnswers: Microsoft Defender External Attack Surface Management (EASM)
explanation: EASM performs outside-in discovery of internet-facing assets, ideal for M&A due diligence.

prompt: Adatum's leadership asks for a single number to track whether the environment's security configuration is improving over time. Which Defender for Cloud metric fits?
choices: The number of enabled Defender plans
The number of regulatory standards enabled
correctAnswers: Secure Score
explanation: Secure Score is the single percentage that trends configuration/posture improvement over time.

prompt: Order the steps to get one unified compliance view across Azure, AWS, and GCP.
reorderItems: Onboard the AWS account as a multicloud connector in Defender for Cloud
Onboard the GCP project as a multicloud connector
Review the unified Secure Score and Inventory across all three
Enable a regulatory standard (e.g. ISO 27001) across the connected environments
explanation: Both clouds must be connected before a unified view exists to review, with additional standards layered on afterward.

prompt: Order the steps to discover a newly acquired subsidiary's unknown internet-facing assets before onboarding it.
reorderItems: Run a Microsoft Defender EASM discovery scan against the subsidiary's known domains
Review the discovered inventory of internet-facing assets
Investigate any unexpected or shadow-IT assets found
Bring the subsidiary's known Azure resources into Defender for Cloud's inventory
explanation: External discovery comes first (outside-in), followed by investigation, then internal onboarding (inside-out) of known resources.

prompt: Configure this simplified Defender for Cloud environment settings screen to extend posture management to a connected AWS account.
explanation: An AWS connector onboards the AWS account, and MCSB is the default benchmark applied to assess it, just as with Azure resources.

prompt: Proposed solution: Track the number of Defender plans enabled each month. Does this solution meet the goal?
correctAnswers: No
explanation: A count of enabled plans measures coverage, not the underlying configuration quality or its trend over time.

prompt: Proposed solution: Track Secure Score over time in Defender for Cloud. Does this solution meet the goal?
correctAnswers: Yes
explanation: Secure Score is exactly the single percentage that trends configuration/posture improvement across connected environments.

prompt: Proposed solution: Track the number of EASM-discovered assets each month. Does this solution meet the goal?
correctAnswers: No
explanation: EASM measures the size of the external attack surface, not the quality of security configuration.
